jokes.text: The worst thing you can do is box a left-hander. Why? They're put on backwards as it is.
jokes.setup: Boxing advice about left-handers being difficult opponents
jokes.punchline: Treating left-handers as literally backwards people, not just southpaw boxers
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay on 'backwards' with perfect economy - boxing advice becomes absurd logic about left-handed people's fundamental nature

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: The worst thing you can do is box a left-hander. Why? They're put on backwards as it is.

jokes.text: Wouldn't that prepare you properly? That's a great idea. I wasn't born yesterday. I never thought about that.
jokes.setup: Larry suggests practicing boxing in a mirror to prepare for left-handers
jokes.punchline: George claims he wasn't born yesterday while admitting he never thought of this obvious solution
jokes.explanation: Good character-based irony - George's contradictory statements reveal his dim-witted nature

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Wouldn't that prepare you properly? That's a great idea.

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: I wasn't born yesterday. I never thought about that.

jokes.text: you'll agree with me... I don't think I should cave in, right? No, not at all. Belly-bump or do something.
jokes.setup: Larry asks for advice about Hank's contract negotiations
jokes.punchline: George applies his wrestling strategy to contract talks
jokes.explanation: Perfect character consistency - George can only see the world through his wrestling lens, even for business advice

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: you'll agree with me... I don't think I should cave in, right?

dialogue.speaker: George
dialogue.line: No, not at all. Belly-bump or do something.

jokes.text: Steam coming out of my ass, Arty.
jokes.setup: Artie commenting on Chef Paul's spicy gumbo
jokes.punchline: Crude but vivid description of digestive consequences
jokes.explanation: Artie's characteristically blunt vulgarity - economical but relies on shock value

dialogue.line: Steam coming out of my ass, Arty.

jokes.text: At the moment, the latest demand is a golf cart to drive around the studio. Poor deluded bastard.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Hank's contract demands
jokes.punchline: Golf cart reveals Hank's inflated sense of importance
jokes.explanation: Perfect character comedy - the golf cart demand is both absurd and perfectly Hank, while Artie's reaction is perfectly blunt

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: At the moment, the latest demand is a golf cart to drive around the studio. Poor deluded bastard.

jokes.text: My dog wouldn't eat that shit.
jokes.setup: Artie apologizing for being affected by the gumbo
jokes.punchline: Crude assessment of the chef's cooking
jokes.explanation: Pure Artie - economical vulgarity that's in character but not particularly clever

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: My dog wouldn't eat that shit.

jokes.text: Do you think that stain on his pants is from the gumbo? I don't think so.
jokes.setup: Artie has been having digestive issues from the gumbo
jokes.punchline: Visual implication of bowel accident with deadpan delivery
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy enhanced by Artie's matter-of-fact delivery - crude but effectively cringe

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Do you think that stain on his pants is from the gumbo? I don't think so.

jokes.text: Kenny Stabler? He played football. Basketball. I didn't have any spare footballs around when he was on the show... so it's the closest kind of ball I could find.
jokes.setup: Hank offers Larry an autographed basketball from Kenny Stabler
jokes.punchline: Hank's illogical solution to not having the right type of ball
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Hank's dim-witted logic and desperate attempts to appear professional while making no sense

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Kenny Stabler? He played football. Basketball. I didn't have any spare footballs around when he was on the show... so it's the closest kind of ball I could find.

jokes.text: How do you place a value on a man who introduces Wynnona Judd as 'TheJudd'?
jokes.setup: Larry asks what Hank thinks he's worth
jokes.punchline: Artie cites Hank's on-air incompetence
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly constructed joke - the setup creates expectation of serious worth assessment, punchline delivers devastating specific example of incompetence with perfect economy

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: How do you place a value on a man who introduces Wynnona Judd as 'TheJudd'?

jokes.text: She was... Jane Russell?
jokes.setup: Discussing Wynonna Judd incident
jokes.punchline: Larry's confused attempt to remember who came out
jokes.explanation: Larry's confusion extends the Hank incompetence theme but lacks punch on its own

dialogue.line: She was... Jane Russell?

jokes.text: Fuck it, it's free cake. We can't miss this.
jokes.setup: Discussion about attending Hank's going-away party
jokes.punchline: Artie's pragmatic motivation for attendance
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - cuts through sentiment with blunt pragmatism, economical and in character

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Fuck it, it's free cake. We can't miss this.

jokes.text: If someone invented a camera that could take, you know, snapshots of our feelings... I would take a picture of this moment and keep it in the wallet of my heart forever.
jokes.setup: Hank giving an emotional farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Overwrought metaphor revealing Hank's pretentious sentimentality
jokes.explanation: Pure Hank - overwrought, self-important sentiment that's both touching and ridiculous. The 'wallet of my heart' is memorably awful

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: If someone invented a camera that could take, you know, snapshots of our feelings... I would take a picture of this moment and keep it in the wallet of my heart forever.

jokes.text: I say he goes right into the cruise director story. Bet you a hundred bucks it's the telephone story.
jokes.setup: Hank beginning another story
jokes.punchline: Staff betting on which of Hank's repetitive stories he'll tell
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-humor showing how predictable Hank has become, while revealing office dynamics and Hank's self-importance

dialogue.line: I say he goes right into the cruise director story.
Bet you a hundred bucks it's the telephone story.

jokes.text: Every time we have a going-away party for you, it's just too much of a downer.
jokes.setup: Introducing a stripper at Hank's party
jokes.punchline: Reveals this is a recurring event, not genuine farewell
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reveal that this is a pattern - recontextualizes the entire party and Hank's dramatics while justifying the stripper

dialogue.line: Every time we have a going-away party for you, it's just too much of a downer.

jokes.text: That number you gave me was to some dry cleaner's in Torrance. Yeah, the area code got changed. Bullshit. I am not your whore.
jokes.setup: Stripper confronting Hank about fake phone number
jokes.punchline: Hank's weak excuse and stripper's indignant response
jokes.explanation: Peak cringe - Hank's pathetic excuse meets professional pride, creating uncomfortable comedy that reveals character

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: That number you gave me was to some dry cleaner's in Torrance.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Yeah, the area code got changed.

dialogue.speaker: Barbara
dialogue.line: Bullshit. I am not your whore.

jokes.text: People see you in enough of those lawn furniture spots... they tend to fantasize about you.
jokes.setup: Awkward aftermath of stripper confrontation
jokes.punchline: Hank's delusional explanation for unwanted attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank delusion - blames his minor commercial work for creating unrealistic expectations rather than his own behavior

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: People see you in enough of those lawn furniture spots... they tend to fantasize about you.

jokes.text: Why do I need this car anyway? I already have a car. 'Cause you deserve it. It's safer, and it's classier.
jokes.setup: Larry on phone with car salesman
jokes.punchline: Salesman's perfectly calibrated response to Larry's insecurity
jokes.explanation: Good observation on sales psychology - Larry's need for validation met with textbook responses

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Why do I need this car anyway? I already have a car.

dialogue.speaker: Car salesman
dialogue.line: 'Cause you deserve it. It's safer, and it's classier.

jokes.text: let Arty and I take care of the strippers... because we can probably find one that doesn't know you.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to make light of the stripper incident
jokes.punchline: Implies Hank has burned through all available strippers
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - Larry's attempt at humor accidentally reveals the scope of Hank's problems with professional women

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: let Arty and I take care of the strippers... because we can probably find one that doesn't know you.

jokes.text: She was half theJudds. Now there's just one of them, so she's a... she is a Judd. She is a singleJudd!
jokes.setup: Hank defending his Wynonna Judd introduction
jokes.punchline: Increasingly desperate logic reaching 'singleJudd'
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation of Hank's flawed logic - 'singleJudd' is both ridiculous and somehow makes sense in Hank's world

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: She was half theJudds. Now there's just one of them, so she's a... she is a Judd. She is a singleJudd!

jokes.text: What about the time I chipped my tooth on the bathroom urinal? What the fuck is so comical about that? It was a back tooth, Hank. I don't know how you did it.
jokes.setup: Hank citing another incident where he was mocked
jokes.punchline: Larry's bewilderment at the physical impossibility
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd physical comedy - the image is both disgusting and geometrically puzzling, perfectly capturing Hank's unique talent for humiliation

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: What about the time I chipped my tooth on the bathroom urinal? What the fuck is so comical about that?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It was a back tooth, Hank. I don't know how you did it.

jokes.text: That was the speaker phone. Nice try.
jokes.setup: Larry trying to blame a fart on the speaker phone
jokes.punchline: Hank's immediate rejection of the excuse
jokes.explanation: Basic physical comedy elevated by the characters' relationship - Larry's desperation and Hank's knowledge of his habits

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: That was the speaker phone. Nice try.

jokes.text: When you let one go, boy. Hello. That was the speaker phone. Nice try.
jokes.setup: Hank accuses Larry of farting
jokes.punchline: Larry's weak excuse about speaker phone feedback
jokes.explanation: Basic bathroom humor elevated slightly by Larry's pathetic attempt to blame technology.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: When you let one go, boy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Hello. That was the speaker phone.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Nice try.

jokes.text: I was eating a sandwich in my office when suddenly I heard you guys.
jokes.setup: Artie caught listening on speaker phone
jokes.punchline: Implausible innocent explanation
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Artie's matter-of-fact delivery of obvious lie fits his personality

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I was eating a sandwich in my office when suddenly I heard you guys.

jokes.text: I'm tired of being your personal village idiot.
jokes.setup: Hank's emotional outburst about being mocked
jokes.punchline: Unexpectedly accurate self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Moment of surprising self-awareness from Hank - the phrase is both accurate and pathetic, showing rare insight

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I'm tired of being your personal village idiot.

jokes.text: If men carried the baby, wouldn't it? He'd be like, 'I got things to do. Get it out! Yeah, right, okay. Yeah, okay, I see it. Come on.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about having a baby
jokes.punchline: Male impatience applied to childbirth
jokes.explanation: Standard Robin Williams observational material - energetic but not particularly original or sharp

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: If men carried the baby, wouldn't it? He'd be like, 'I got things to do. Get it out! Yeah, right, okay. Yeah, okay, I see it. Come on.'

jokes.text: It's just too damn easy. He looks like a before from Hair Club. I can't go with you on that. I cannot.
jokes.setup: Robin making jokes about Hank
jokes.punchline: Larry's protective response to guest mocking his sidekick
jokes.explanation: Good character dynamics - Larry's professional boundaries conflict with his personal feelings about Hank

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: It's just too damn easy. He looks like a before from Hair Club.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I can't go with you on that. I cannot.

jokes.text: my head reminded you of your penis after a cold swim... I was honored.
jokes.setup: Larry recalling Robin's insult
jokes.punchline: Larry's masochistic appreciation of the insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry neurosis - finding honor in being cleverly insulted by a celebrity guest

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: my head reminded you of your penis after a cold swim... I was honored.

jokes.text: He's hung like a field mouse. It's like a little sundial. Honey, what time is it? Get the baby. It's 3:00. Oh, 2:30 now. It's real cold.
jokes.setup: Talking about his baby
jokes.punchline: Elaborate sundial metaphor for small penis
jokes.explanation: Classic Robin Williams - takes basic concept and adds elaborate metaphorical extension, but relies on crude subject matter

dialogue.speaker: Robin Williams
dialogue.line: He's hung like a field mouse. It's like a little sundial. Honey, what time is it? Get the baby. It's 3:00. Oh, 2:30 now. It's real cold.

jokes.text: Don't you know? No, my TV stops at channel 13... the way it's supposed to.
jokes.setup: Discussion about cable channels
jokes.punchline: Artie's old-fashioned resistance to cable TV
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie curmudgeonly attitude - treats technological progress as moral failing with typical bluntness

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Don't you know? No, my TV stops at channel 13... the way it's supposed to.

jokes.text: Hey, you untalented, overpriced sidekick... you're just another piece of meat to us... so why don't you take what we gave you last year and be happy with that... you worthless piece of shit?
jokes.setup: Hank recounting what the network supposedly said
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment delivered as victim's perspective
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Hank's version of events is simultaneously self-pitying and probably accurate about his value

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hey, you untalented, overpriced sidekick... you're just another piece of meat to us... so why don't you take what we gave you last year and be happy with that... you worthless piece of shit?

jokes.text: There's no Cavett. You hear me? Zip on Cavett. I'm bluffing.
jokes.setup: Larry asking about the Cavett offer
jokes.punchline: Hank's complete admission of having no leverage
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Hank's earlier bravado - 'Zip on Cavett' is economical and devastatingly honest

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: There's no Cavett. You hear me? Zip on Cavett. I'm bluffing.

jokes.text: It's that golf cart I told you about last week. I smell urine here.
jokes.setup: Explaining the 'Hankmobile'
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of venue's unsanitary condition
jokes.explanation: Builds atmosphere of seedy meeting location while maintaining focus on absurd contract demand

dialogue.speaker: Shel
dialogue.line: It's that golf cart I told you about last week.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: I smell urine here.

jokes.text: I don't mean on these stairs. I mean, you know, the network.
jokes.setup: Larry saying he loves being 'here'
jokes.punchline: Clarifying he doesn't mean the urine-soaked stairwell
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that acknowledges the absurdity of their meeting location while maintaining dramatic tension

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I don't mean on these stairs. I mean, you know, the network.

jokes.text: I had to pay for it myself, so don't tell anybody, okay?
jokes.setup: Hank showing off his new golf cart
jokes.punchline: Reveals he bought his own 'perk' to maintain appearance
jokes.explanation: Devastating character revelation - Hank's desperation to appear successful leads to self-financed fake victory. Perfect pathetic comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I had to pay for it myself, so don't tell anybody, okay?

jokes.text: You got a very different way of showing your love, don't you? I've seen men be burned in dark interiors.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to get emotional with Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry deflecting with absurd car interior safety advice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment - avoiding emotional intimacy with ridiculous practical concerns.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: You got a very different way of showing your love, don't you?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I've seen men be burned in dark interiors.

jokes.text: You did have that offer to go on after Nightline, didn't you? Fuck no.
jokes.setup: Artie questioning Larry's leverage claim
jokes.punchline: Larry's blunt admission of his own bluff
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback and character revelation - Larry's own hypocrisy exposed with brutal economy in final line

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You did have that offer to go on after Nightline, didn't you?

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Fuck no.
